title: Licensing-ideology split candidate
notes: Candidate representation generated after the first adversarial pass. It is not scored as strong; it is meant to test whether community licensing can be kept distinct from standard-language ideology, correction pressure, and reported acceptability.

edges.rationale: Speaker cues can shift judgment responses, especially where a form is socially indexed or dialectally marked.
Genre conditions which forms count as contextually appropriate without by itself deciding grammatical status.
Register mismatch can lower reported acceptability even when a form remains licensed in its community.
Judgment instructions can cue school-grammar, dialect-tolerance, or communicative-success criteria.
Ideological norm-centres can generate explicit condemnation of forms independently of community licensing.
Prestige norms can depress acceptability reports for stigmatized but licensed forms.
Explicit condemnation predicts correction in edited or pedagogical contexts.
Forms licensed by a community should be available for production within the relevant contexts.
Community licensing is evidence for grammaticality attribution, but the candidate does not identify it with the attribution itself.
Correction behaviour can inform attribution, while remaining separable from licensing because correction can be ideological.
Acceptability reports are evidence about grammaticality attribution, not a direct measurement of grammaticality.
